title: Hyperthyroidism — Graves'
oneLiner: Autoimmune stimulation of TSH receptor causing thyrotoxicosis; common in young women; key risks are thyroid storm and atrial fibrillation.

sections.Hyperthyroidism — Graves' Disease: **One-liner:** Autoimmune stimulation of TSH receptor causing thyrotoxicosis; common in young women; key risks are thyroid storm and atrial fibrillation.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Thyroid storm** — fever >38°C + tachycardia >140/min + delirium/coma; treat with beta-blocker, antithyroid, steroids, cooling — do NOT wait for labs.
- **Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response** — uncontrolled AF can cause haemodynamic collapse; rate control with IV beta-blocker is urgent, even before TFT results.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Weight loss despite increased appetite
  - Palpitations, heat intolerance, sweating
  - Tremor, anxiety, irritability
  - Fatigue, proximal muscle weakness
  - Increased frequency of bowel movements
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Diffuse goitre (often with bruit on auscultation)
  - Tachycardia, bounding pulse, AF
  - Fine tremor of outstretched hands
  - Warm, moist skin
  - Graves’ eye signs: lid lag (von Graefe's sign), lid retraction, proptosis, periorbital oedema
  - Pretibial myxedema (rare)
  - Thyroid acropachy (digital clubbing, rare)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Toxic multinodular goitre** — age >50, nodular goitre, no eye signs
  - **De Quervain’s (subacute) thyroiditis** — painful goitre, preceding viral illness, low uptake on scan
  - **Silent (painless) thyroiditis** — postpartum, no goitre tenderness, low uptake
  - **Facetitious thyrotoxicosis** — exogenous thyroxine use, low/absent goitre
  - **Phaeochromocytoma** — episodic hypertension, headache, sweating, no goitre, raised metanephrines
- **Splitter:** **Thyroid uptake scan** — high uptake in Graves’ / toxic nodular goitre vs. low uptake in thyroiditis / factitious.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG — check for AF, sinus tachycardia, rate control
  - HR, BP, temperature, oxygen saturations
- **Bloods:**
  - TSH (suppressed) + free T4 (+ free T3 if T4 normal)
  - Full blood count (ritual before starting antithyroid drugs — risk of agranulocytosis)
  - Liver function tests (baseline, especially if using PTU)
  - Anti-TPO, anti-Tg antibodies (support autoimmune background)
  - TRAb (TSH receptor antibodies) — confirm Grave’s if clinical diagnosis uncertain
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Thyroid uptake scan** (99mTc-pertechnetate) — diffuse increased uptake in Grave’s; second-line: ultrasound if nodule suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - If eye signs: **formal ophthalmology assessment** (clinical activity score + exophthalmometry)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Clinical diagnosis** — high pretest probability + suppressed TSH + raised T4/T3 + TRAb ± diffuse uptake on scan.
- **Never-miss test:** **TSH + free T4** — without them, you cannot confirm hyperthyroidism or differentiate from secondary causes.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If thyroid storm suspected: IV propranolol (1-2 mg slow bolus, repeat as needed) + IV hydrocortisone (100 mg) + oral carbimazole (60-80 mg loading) — use nasogastric tube if unable to swallow.
  - Symptomatic tachycardia without storm: oral propranolol 40-80 mg TDS.
  - Start carbimazole 20-40 mg daily (or propylthiouracil 100-150 mg TDS if pregnant in first trimester).
  - Administer iodine (oral Lugol’s iodine or SSKI) only AFTER antithyroid drug started — to reduce thyroid hormone release.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue propranolol BID/QDS until euthyroid (usually 2-4 weeks).
  - Monitor TFTs weekly; carbimazole dose adjustment based on T4.
  - Check FBC at baseline and then if any fever/sore throat (risk of agranulocytosis).
  - Refer to endocrinology.
  - If AF: anticoagulate if CHA₂DS₂‑VASc ≥1 (after ruling out valvular cause) — discuss haematology if high bleeding risk.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antithyroid drug therapy (carbimazole) for 12-18 months; then trial off to see if remission.
  - Radioactive iodine (I-131) — first-line for relapse / contraindications to drugs / elderly.
  - Thyroidectomy — if large goitre, pressure symptoms, suspicious nodule, or patient preference.
  - Ophthalmopathy: smoking cessation, selenium supplementation (if mild), refer for steroids/orbital radiotherapy if active moderate-severe.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid if dehydrated (e.g., Hartmann’s 1L/8h).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (adjusted for renal function / weight).
  - Diet: normal, high-calorie, avoid caffeine.
  - Observation: 4-hourly NEWS2, ECG monitoring if AF.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Carbimazole** — initial 20-40 mg PO once daily (propranolol added for symptoms).
    - Maintenance: 5-15 mg OD.
    - Duration: 12-18 months then trial off.
  - **Propranolol** — 40-80 mg PO TDS (or IV 1-2 mg slow bolus in storm). Taper over 2-4 weeks as euthyroid.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Propylthiouracil (PTU)** — 100-150 mg PO TDS; preferred in first trimester of pregnancy (less teratogenic than carbimazole).
  - **Radioactive iodine (I-131)** — dose per local protocol; contraindicated in pregnancy, breastfeeding, active ophthalmopathy, children.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Carbimazole: caution in hepatic impairment, avoid if severe agranulocytosis history.
  - Propranolol: caution in asthma (can worsen bronchospasm) — use cardioselective beta-blocker (atenolol) if needed.
  - PTU: hepatotoxicity (black box warning); monitor LFTs.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No major renal adjustment for carbimazole/PTU.
  - PTU — reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment; avoid if baseline LFTs >3x ULN.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Carbimazole / PTU — starting without baseline FBC and failing to advise patient about agranulocytosis** (sore throat, fever, mouth ulcers — stop drug immediately and check FBC). Leading cause of preventable harm.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Thyroid storm (decompensated hyperthyroidism with multi-organ failure)
  - Atrial fibrillation with embolic stroke
  - Agranulocytosis (carbimazole/PTU) — usually within first 3 months
  - Hepatotoxicity (PTU > carbimazole)
- **Chronic:**
  - Graves’ ophthalmopathy (can worsen with radioiodine or smoking)
  - Hypothyroidism after radioiodine or surgery
  - Relapse after drug therapy (40-60% within 2 years)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Remission rate ~40-60% after 12-18 months of antithyroid drugs; higher in mild disease, small goitre, low TRAb levels.**
- **Without treatment: risk of cardiac morbidity, bone loss, and storm (mortality 10-25% even with treatment).**
- **After radioiodine or surgery: >90% become hypothyroid (lifelong levothyroxine).**
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document smoking status** — smoking worsens ophthalmopathy; advise cessation.
- **In any young patient with AF** — check TFTs (Graves’ is a common reversible cause).
- **Refer all patients with eye signs to ophthalmology** — early treatment of active orbitopathy improves outcomes.
- **Check TRAb if considering radioiodine** — high titres increase risk of worsening eye disease.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start beta-blocker without starting antithyroid drug** — will not treat underlying hyperthyroidism; risk of precipitating hypotension in storm.
- **Do not give iodine without first giving antithyroid drug** — will exacerbate thyrotoxicosis (Wolff-Chaikoff escape).
- **Do not forget to check FBC if patient calls about fever/sore throat after starting carbimazole** — agranulocytosis is a medical emergency.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers (stop carbimazole/PTU, get urgent FBC)
  - Palpitations, chest pain, shortness of breath
  - Worsening eye symptoms (pain, double vision, vision loss)
  - Jaundice, dark urine, abdominal pain (PTU hepatotoxicity)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Endocrine outpatient clinic within 2-4 weeks (sooner if unstable)
  - Check TFTs at 4-6 weeks after starting, then every 2-3 months until euthyroid
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / endocrinology registrar for:**
  - Suspected thyroid storm (fever + tachycardia + altered mental state)
  - Heart rate >150/min not controlled with beta-blocker
  - Atrial fibrillation with rapid rate >140/min despite treatment
  - Agranulocytosis (WBC <1.0 or neutrophils <0.5) — stop drug, start broad-spectrum antibiotics, consult haematology
  - Clinical deterioration with NEWS2 ≥5 or increasing
- **Call ICU if:**
  - GCS <12, shock, need for vasopressors, significant arrhythmia, or intubation required
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation

rawMarkdown:
## Hyperthyroidism — Graves' Disease

**One-liner:** Autoimmune stimulation of TSH receptor causing thyrotoxicosis; common in young women; key risks are thyroid storm and atrial fibrillation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Thyroid storm** — fever >38°C + tachycardia >140/min + delirium/coma; treat with beta-blocker, antithyroid, steroids, cooling — do NOT wait for labs.
- **Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response** — uncontrolled AF can cause haemodynamic collapse; rate control with IV beta-blocker is urgent, even before TFT results.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Weight loss despite increased appetite
  - Palpitations, heat intolerance, sweating
  - Tremor, anxiety, irritability
  - Fatigue, proximal muscle weakness
  - Increased frequency of bowel movements
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Diffuse goitre (often with bruit on auscultation)
  - Tachycardia, bounding pulse, AF
  - Fine tremor of outstretched hands
  - Warm, moist skin
  - Graves’ eye signs: lid lag (von Graefe's sign), lid retraction, proptosis, periorbital oedema
  - Pretibial myxedema (rare)
  - Thyroid acropachy (digital clubbing, rare)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Toxic multinodular goitre** — age >50, nodular goitre, no eye signs
  - **De Quervain’s (subacute) thyroiditis** — painful goitre, preceding viral illness, low uptake on scan
  - **Silent (painless) thyroiditis** — postpartum, no goitre tenderness, low uptake
  - **Facetitious thyrotoxicosis** — exogenous thyroxine use, low/absent goitre
  - **Phaeochromocytoma** — episodic hypertension, headache, sweating, no goitre, raised metanephrines
- **Splitter:** **Thyroid uptake scan** — high uptake in Graves’ / toxic nodular goitre vs. low uptake in thyroiditis / factitious.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG — check for AF, sinus tachycardia, rate control
  - HR, BP, temperature, oxygen saturations
- **Bloods:**
  - TSH (suppressed) + free T4 (+ free T3 if T4 normal)
  - Full blood count (ritual before starting antithyroid drugs — risk of agranulocytosis)
  - Liver function tests (baseline, especially if using PTU)
  - Anti-TPO, anti-Tg antibodies (support autoimmune background)
  - TRAb (TSH receptor antibodies) — confirm Grave’s if clinical diagnosis uncertain
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: **Thyroid uptake scan** (99mTc-pertechnetate) — diffuse increased uptake in Grave’s; second-line: ultrasound if nodule suspected.
- **Special tests:**
  - If eye signs: **formal ophthalmology assessment** (clinical activity score + exophthalmometry)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **Clinical diagnosis** — high pretest probability + suppressed TSH + raised T4/T3 + TRAb ± diffuse uptake on scan.
- **Never-miss test:** **TSH + free T4** — without them, you cannot confirm hyperthyroidism or differentiate from secondary causes.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If thyroid storm suspected: IV propranolol (1-2 mg slow bolus, repeat as needed) + IV hydrocortisone (100 mg) + oral carbimazole (60-80 mg loading) — use nasogastric tube if unable to swallow.
  - Symptomatic tachycardia without storm: oral propranolol 40-80 mg TDS.
  - Start carbimazole 20-40 mg daily (or propylthiouracil 100-150 mg TDS if pregnant in first trimester).
  - Administer iodine (oral Lugol’s iodine or SSKI) only AFTER antithyroid drug started — to reduce thyroid hormone release.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue propranolol BID/QDS until euthyroid (usually 2-4 weeks).
  - Monitor TFTs weekly; carbimazole dose adjustment based on T4.
  - Check FBC at baseline and then if any fever/sore throat (risk of agranulocytosis).
  - Refer to endocrinology.
  - If AF: anticoagulate if CHA₂DS₂‑VASc ≥1 (after ruling out valvular cause) — discuss haematology if high bleeding risk.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Antithyroid drug therapy (carbimazole) for 12-18 months; then trial off to see if remission.
  - Radioactive iodine (I-131) — first-line for relapse / contraindications to drugs / elderly.
  - Thyroidectomy — if large goitre, pressure symptoms, suspicious nodule, or patient preference.
  - Ophthalmopathy: smoking cessation, selenium supplementation (if mild), refer for steroids/orbital radiotherapy if active moderate-severe.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: IV crystalloid if dehydrated (e.g., Hartmann’s 1L/8h).
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (adjusted for renal function / weight).
  - Diet: normal, high-calorie, avoid caffeine.
  - Observation: 4-hourly NEWS2, ECG monitoring if AF.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Carbimazole** — initial 20-40 mg PO once daily (propranolol added for symptoms).
    - Maintenance: 5-15 mg OD.
    - Duration: 12-18 months then trial off.
  - **Propranolol** — 40-80 mg PO TDS (or IV 1-2 mg slow bolus in storm). Taper over 2-4 weeks as euthyroid.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Propylthiouracil (PTU)** — 100-150 mg PO TDS; preferred in first trimester of pregnancy (less teratogenic than carbimazole).
  - **Radioactive iodine (I-131)** — dose per local protocol; contraindicated in pregnancy, breastfeeding, active ophthalmopathy, children.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Carbimazole: caution in hepatic impairment, avoid if severe agranulocytosis history.
  - Propranolol: caution in asthma (can worsen bronchospasm) — use cardioselective beta-blocker (atenolol) if needed.
  - PTU: hepatotoxicity (black box warning); monitor LFTs.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No major renal adjustment for carbimazole/PTU.
  - PTU — reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment; avoid if baseline LFTs >3x ULN.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Carbimazole / PTU — starting without baseline FBC and failing to advise patient about agranulocytosis** (sore throat, fever, mouth ulcers — stop drug immediately and check FBC). Leading cause of preventable harm.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Thyroid storm (decompensated hyperthyroidism with multi-organ failure)
  - Atrial fibrillation with embolic stroke
  - Agranulocytosis (carbimazole/PTU) — usually within first 3 months
  - Hepatotoxicity (PTU > carbimazole)
- **Chronic:**
  - Graves’ ophthalmopathy (can worsen with radioiodine or smoking)
  - Hypothyroidism after radioiodine or surgery
  - Relapse after drug therapy (40-60% within 2 years)

### Prognosis
- **Remission rate ~40-60% after 12-18 months of antithyroid drugs; higher in mild disease, small goitre, low TRAb levels.**
- **Without treatment: risk of cardiac morbidity, bone loss, and storm (mortality 10-25% even with treatment).**
- **After radioiodine or surgery: >90% become hypothyroid (lifelong levothyroxine).**

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document smoking status** — smoking worsens ophthalmopathy; advise cessation.
- **In any young patient with AF** — check TFTs (Graves’ is a common reversible cause).
- **Refer all patients with eye signs to ophthalmology** — early treatment of active orbitopathy improves outcomes.
- **Check TRAb if considering radioiodine** — high titres increase risk of worsening eye disease.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start beta-blocker without starting antithyroid drug** — will not treat underlying hyperthyroidism; risk of precipitating hypotension in storm.
- **Do not give iodine without first giving antithyroid drug** — will exacerbate thyrotoxicosis (Wolff-Chaikoff escape).
- **Do not forget to check FBC if patient calls about fever/sore throat after starting carbimazole** — agranulocytosis is a medical emergency.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers (stop carbimazole/PTU, get urgent FBC)
  - Palpitations, chest pain, shortness of breath
  - Worsening eye symptoms (pain, double vision, vision loss)
  - Jaundice, dark urine, abdominal pain (PTU hepatotoxicity)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Endocrine outpatient clinic within 2-4 weeks (sooner if unstable)
  - Check TFTs at 4-6 weeks after starting, then every 2-3 months until euthyroid

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / endocrinology registrar for:**
  - Suspected thyroid storm (fever + tachycardia + altered mental state)
  - Heart rate >150/min not controlled with beta-blocker
  - Atrial fibrillation with rapid rate >140/min despite treatment
  - Agranulocytosis (WBC <1.0 or neutrophils <0.5) — stop drug, start broad-spectrum antibiotics, consult haematology
  - Clinical deterioration with NEWS2 ≥5 or increasing
- **Call ICU if:**
  - GCS <12, shock, need for vasopressors, significant arrhythmia, or intubation required
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation
